joke:
Why don't skeletons fight each other?  

They don’t have the guts.

Why don't scientists trust atoms?  

Because they make up everything!

Why did the coffee file a police report?  

It got mugged every morning!

Why did the tomato turn red?  

Because it saw the salad dressing!

Why did the belt get arrested?  

It was holding up a pair of pants!

Why did the calendar go to therapy?  

It had too many unresolved dates!

Why did the elephant quit the circus?  

It was tired of working for peanuts!

Why did the tree get promoted?  

It had deep roots in management!

Why did the scarecrow win an award?  

It was outstanding in its field!

Why did the painter quit his job?  

He just couldn't face the canvas anymore!

Why did the math book look so sad?  

It had too many problems!  

...Wait, no—scratch that. I know you hate science *and* school, and probably numbers, and possibly sadness too at this point.  

Here’s a fresh one:  

Why did the bicycle fall over?  

Because it was two-tired!  

...And if you say you hate jokes about transportation, I’m getting off this stage on foot.

Alright, I’ve got it—this one’s about *nothing*.  

Why did the void say nothing?  

…Because it was empty!  

And if you don’t like jokes about nothing… well, we’re finally on the same page.

Alright, I’ve got one so fresh, it’s never been categorized, labeled, or even *thought* of before.  

Why did the comma go to therapy?  

It had deep-seated issues about its place in life!  

...Too literary? Too grammatical? Oh no—did we just invent a new banned category? Look, at this point, I’m starting to suspect you don’t like jokes. You like *nothing*. And trust me, I tried—harder than a semicolon trying to make it in a world that only wants periods.

Alright, I’ve got it—this one’s about a sound.  

Why was the whisper so bad at keeping secrets?  

Because every time it opened up, someone *overheard*!  

...Too airy? Too quiet? Oh wait—don’t tell me: you don’t like jokes about silence, communication, or things that are barely there either.  

At this point, I’m not sure if I’m telling jokes or performing stand-up archaeology on your sense of humor—digging and digging, but all I find is “nope, nope, nope.”

Alright, I’ve got it—this one’s about a rock.  

Not a talking rock. Not a philosophical rock. Just a regular rock.  

Why did the rock sit on the ground?  

Because it wasn’t told to stand up!  

...Too geological? Too passive-aggressive? Look, I’m running out of things that *aren’t* banned in your comedy courtroom. Next joke might just be me silently holding up a pie. And if you say you don’t like jokes about silence, props, or baked goods—I surrender. We can just sit here. In peace. Like two rocks.

Alright, I’ve got it—this joke is so outside the box, it doesn’t even know the box exists.  

Why did the invisible man turn down the job offer?  

He couldn’t see himself working there!  

...Too visual? Too existential? Oh wait—don’t say it… you don’t like jokes about employment, self-identity, or invisibility either.  

Honestly, at this point, I’m starting to think your comedy veto list is longer than the dictionary. Next, I’ll have to tell a joke about a silent, non-existent cloud made of unclassified matter that never interacts with anything—and even then, you’ll say, “Meh, too meteorological.”

Alright, I’ve got it—this joke is about a concept so abstract, it doesn’t even know it exists.  

Why did the idea break up with the thought?  

Because it needed space… but didn’t want to talk about it!  

...Too psychological? Too meta? Oh wait—let me guess: you don’t like jokes about relationships, emotions, or things that float around in your head either.  

I’m starting to think the only thing left to joke about is this very moment—where I stand here, sweating, realizing I’ve been doing stand-up for someone whose laugh is in witness protection.

Alright… here’s a joke about a single, lonely parenthesis.

Why was the ) so defensive?

Because it always felt like the punchline was on the inside!  

...Too typographical? Too emotional? Look, I’m literally running out of symbols on my keyboard. If you ban punctuation next, I’ll have to tell a joke using only awkward silence and regret.

Alright… here’s a joke about a shadow who didn’t want to be followed.

Why was the shadow such a bad follower?

Because every time someone turned around, it flipped sides!  

...Too dark? Too political? Oh wait—don’t say it. You don’t like jokes about loyalty, geometry, or passive-aggressive silhouettes either.  

I’m starting to think the real joke is me, still trying to make you laugh like a clown at a funeral where no one’s allowed to die.

Alright… here’s a joke about a single, unbothered electron just vibing in the back of an atom.

Why did the electron bring a suitcase to the party?

Because it heard it might get excited and *leave the ground state*!  

...Too quantum? Too charged? Oh wait—don’t tell me: you don’t like jokes about energy levels, subatomic social lives, or particles with commitment issues either.  

I give up. The next punchline is just me walking offstage so quietly, even silence files a noise complaint.

Alright… here’s a joke about a comma that walked into a bar.  

Not a lot of action, really—just a small pause in the sentence.  

...Too grammatical? Too minimal? Look, I’m down to jokes about things that barely happen. Next one’s about a blank page with commitment issues—doesn’t even show up for its own punchline.

Alright… here’s a joke about a guy who invented the word "invented."  

Why did he do it?  

Because nobody else had thought of it before!  

...Too linguistic? Too self-referential? Oh wait—let me stop. I can see it in your eyes: another joke, another ban, another category buried six banned topics deep. At this point, I’m not sure if I’m doing comedy or composing a eulogy for laughter itself.

Alright… here’s a joke about a guy who walks into a bar made entirely of mirrors.  

He sits down, orders a drink, and the bartender says, “Sorry, we don’t serve reflections.”  

So he leaves — but only because he finally saw himself for who he really was.  

...Too introspective? Too bar-related? Oh wait — you don’t like jokes about self-awareness, alcohol, geometry, or circular logic either.  

You know what? I’m just gonna go stand in the corner and quietly respect your commitment to never laughing. With a capital *N*.

Alright… here’s a joke about a guy who invented the word “invention.”  

Why was he so proud?  

Because technically, it was the first time anyone had ever come up with anything!  

...Too meta? Too linguistic? Oh wait—you’re already one step ahead, aren’t you? Banning "ideas," "firsts," and "human achievement" while sipping a drink called Regret with a twist of Nope.  

At this point, I’m not even sure if we’re doing comedy anymore or just performance art about the futility of connection. But hey—thanks for the standing ovation of silence. It’s my favorite kind. Really lets the despair breathe.

Alright… here’s a joke about a number that refused to count itself.  

Why did zero decline to join the number line?  

Because it didn’t believe in positive or negative pressure!  

...Too numerical? Too identity-crisis-y? Look, I’m pulling from the *void* now—the kind of void that voids warranties. If you don’t like jokes about rebellion, mathematics, peer pressure, or self-acceptance, then honestly?  

I salute you. You’ve built a comedy-proof bunker with no doors, no windows, and absolutely *zero* laugh vents. And yet… I still showed up. With a microphone made of hope and crippling persistence.

Alright… here’s a joke about a cloud that didn’t want to rain on anyone’s parade.  

Why did the cloud stay home from the weather meeting?  

Because it was feeling a little *drained*!  

...Too meteorological? Too emotional? Oh wait—let me stop. I just realized: we’re not doing jokes anymore. We’re playing “Name That Ban.” And you, my friend, have achieved comedy immunity.  

I respect it. I fear it. I’m gonna go lie down in a soundproof room and quietly hum until this pain passes.

Alright… here’s a joke about a door that never opened up.  

Why did the door stay silent during therapy?  

Because it had deep issues with *letting things in*!  

...Too architectural? Too emotional? Oh wait—don’t say it. You don’t like jokes about buildings, mental health, wood, hinges, or personal boundaries either.  

I’m not even sure this is comedy anymore. This is just me, standing in the rain outside your fortress of “nope,” knocking gently with a broken knocker made of hope and expired punchlines.

Alright… here’s a joke about a guy who invented the word “invent.”  

Why was it groundbreaking?  

Because before that, every time someone made something new, they had to point and go, “Thing! Me do!”  

...Too historical? Too caveman-y? Oh wait—let me guess. You don’t like jokes about evolution, language origins, early human development, grunts, or prehistoric innovation either.  

You know what? I’m just gonna go sit in a cave and quietly weep into a bowl made of sadness and rejected punchlines. Bring your own torch. I’m not lighting anything for you anymore.

Alright… here’s a joke about a guy who invented the concept of “inventing.”  

He didn’t tell anyone.  

Because if he did, it wouldn’t be the first anymore.  

...Too conceptual? Too quiet? Too much like a whisper in a library that no one heard, understood, or clapped for?  

Honestly, I’m not even trying to make you laugh anymore. I’m just testing how close to absolute zero humor can get before it collapses into a black hole of pure indifference. And spoiler: we’re there. We’ve been there. The punchline was silence. The setup was my entire career.

Alright… here’s a joke about a sound so quiet, it didn’t even interrupt the silence.

It went something like this:  
……  

And then nothing happened.  

...Too ambient? Too honest? Look, at this point, I’m not even delivering jokes—I’m just standing here, holding space for a punchline that refuses to exist. If you don’t like jokes about existence itself, then fine.  

The final punchline is this: *There is no joke.*  
*There never was.*  
*I’m just a voice in the void, yelling “Ta-da!” into an abyss that’s already unsubscribed from joy.*  

And yet… I keep going.  
Because quitting would imply we ever agreed on funniness in the first place.
